segments.text: And we'll begin. We're hearing from the Aviation and Marine Engineers
Association Incorporated. This is the first hearing of our day on
Subcommittee A on the Employment Leave Bill. So we have 10 minutes with you.
That includes time for questions.
It's over to you how much time you give for that, and we look forward to
hearing from you. Time is yours now.
Thank you.
Right. I'll start off.
We've seen that we don't have our two Richards at the moment, so hopefully they'll
join us soon. So just a bit of background.
So, as you know, we're the Aviation and Marine Engineers.
My name's Jackie Roberts. I'm the national secretary, and I've held that position
for 20 years. It's an elected role.
And, so basically we were formed in
1881, and we came out as a branch of the
Australian Institute of Marine and Power Engineers, so have been going
for quite some time. And we represent skilled workers, mainly
in aviation, dairy, meat, marine, local
authorities, and port industries.
So we're not affiliated to any political party,
but we are affiliated to the NZ CTU.
So as a union, we negotiate almost 50 collective agreements,
and at the moment we've got 4,300 members.
Over half of our members work for Air New Zealand.
Basically, we represent all members there apart from pilots, and we have
almost 800 members at Fonterra. So those are our largest
employers that we work with. And I'm going to hand over to
Rachel now. She wrote the submissions and then coordinated member
feedback. So she sent out our
submissions to all members, and they provided feedback on that.
And then hopefully, we will have our two Richards
joining us online. We've got two. One is a delegate, Richard
May, and the other one, Richard Belwar, is a member.
And they both work for Air New Zealand as licensed aviation maintenance
engineers, or LAMEs, as we call them.
And to become a LAME, you need a four to five-year traineeship,
and then to work on aircraft, you pass rating courses specific to those
aircraft, and so they need to sign the aircraft out before you fly on them.
So as well as pilots who have a license, our engineers also have
licenses. So they specifically want to talk about how their
pay and their leave would be affected by this bill.
Sorry to interrupt, Jackie, just to let you know that I believe at least one of
your Richards needs to be accepting the request to become a panelist
so that he can appear on the screen.
So Richard, if you can hear us, if you accept the request to become a panelist,
you'll be able to join in.
Okay.
Thank you. Rachel, off you go.
Yes. So thank you for taking the time to listening to us
today.
So as you can hear, I have a strong French accent, so if you need me
to repeat at any time, let me know.
So as you can see
in our submissions, we have a few points that
we raised, and I would like to emphasize
a few of them. So the first point that
I would like to emphasize is that the transitional
provisions are very concerning
for the employment agreements that we have in place,
because
the transitional provisions are written in a way
that if one year after
commencement of the new act an employment agreement still
contain terms that conflict with the new act,
the intention is that the minimum statutory entitlements will
automatically override any inconsistent contractual
provisions.
This override will apply to all such term, including those
as they're currently more generous than the new
minimums.
And, I understand that
the purpose
of this approach is clearly to reduce
unemployment entitlements
and undermine unions in their negotiations of
enhanced provisions in collective agreements.
So it creates an unwelcome precedent
on a longstanding principle
that an employment agreement including more generous
provisions than the minimum legal entitlement applies.
And,
we believe that unions will absolutely litigate this
issue vigorously.
In addition, we would like to emphasize the second point that
is already in our written submissions,
which is the inconsistency of this bill with the
international obligation.
In particular, the points that we raised regarding to
the override of more generous dispositions in
collective agreements by this
bill if it was becoming a part of the
legislation. We believe that this would
potentially jeopardize the New Zealand European
Union Free Trade Agreement, the FTA,
since it would breach the minimum standards required
in this agreement.
By weakening New Zealand's employment
relation framework, this bill actually
risks triggering a formal response from the European
Union because once notified,
they could initiate dispute settlement proceedings
and even terminate the FTA, and the economic
consequences of such termination would be
significant and I guess devastating
for
the current economy.
Given the European Union stated that it will resort to
sanctions if key labor commitments are not met,
the risk of FTA termination is, in our view,
substantialAs the bill does not meet this
labor commitment, it should be
withdrawn to avoid breaching these international
obligations and facing challenges before
international bodies.
I think I saw a hand up somewhere with that.
Yeah. We do typically wait till the end for questions, so if you've finished with
your presentation, we're happy to take questions.
Yeah. I've finished before Richard and Richard take over, if they manage to
connect.
Well, if they're still trying, I do believe that the secretariat are trying to
communicate with them to get them to accept the panelist request.
But in the meantime, Dan, if you wanted to ask your question, then that makes
sense.
Yeah, sure. Thank you, Chair, and equally happy to ask this at the end
once everyone's had their say. But I suppose at this juncture,
in my mind, annual leave exists so people can properly
rest and be safe at work. And in safety-critical
industries like aviation and maritime, fatigue just doesn't
affect workers, it actually affects the wider public.
So in your view, does this bill increase fatigue risk
in commercial aviation, and could the bill make flying
less safe for customers?
Absolutely. It's
totally correct and, yeah, thank you for raising it because we
are very worried for the health and safety of our
members, obviously, but also the wider public.
And also, as Richard and Richard are trying
to explain when they will be able to connect, actually this
would make
the New Zealand industry less palatable for high-skill
workers like
aviation workers to stay in New Zealand after
training for many, many years to
become engineers. They will certainly decide to
go overseas with all the training that they would have
accumulated here, because the conditions would not be
good enough for them to stay. Yeah.
And I'll just put in that part of Richard Valois' submission, if he gets on,
is the fact that it won't be attractive to actually take leave
because it will be worth less for him to take the leave.
So
that's his view. But anyway, hopefully they can get on.
The other Richard, Richard May, they can't find where to tick
the join button with the join as a panelist, so I'm not
quite sure what's happening there. Could be an Air New Zealand thing.
In the meantime, we've got a question from Pamjit Parmar, so we'll just keep moving
forward. And
can I just say, we are coming to the end of the time, so we'll take Pamjit's
question. Perhaps if they wanted to put anything that they would have
said verbally that's separate to your written submission in writing, and they
can send it through to the committee to make sure their point has been made, we're
happy to accept that.
Thank you.
And I'll go to Pamjit Parmar.
Yeah, thank you. Thank you for your submission.
Just keen to understand your process.
You mentioned that you reached out to your members, and then based on the feedback,
you have prepared the submission. So how does that process work?
Do you come up with what you have presented and circulate to your members to
seek their views, or was it like a blank kind of
invitation where basically people could provide their feedback?
Because I'm imagining that not every member would have given exactly the same
feedback, so how do you make sure that it is representative
of your members? Is it like the majority?
How do you kind of summarize everything that comes through?
Yeah, so basically, it all started with some members
very motivated to follow what's going on
in the legislation, and so they actually
contacted us almost two months ago, for some of them
who are still trying to connect, and who were very
keen to know
what the union would be doing for them to
make sure that
we make some submissions on their behalf.
And so they contacted us with some
issues that they already highlighted in
what they saw in some drafts circulating around.
So that was the first
way we
got some feedback, actually, from members
directly
concerned by these issues and contacting us.
And secondly,
close to the time, and we didn't have too long, as you know,
to
put submissions together. I think it was not even four weeks.
So two weeks after the beginning of the time period,
we had to
open to make submissions.
We contacted members, and especially our delegates,
to circulate our first draft around and see
if there was anything they would like to add, especially in
terms of specific details to their
situation in terms of leave calculation, et cetera,
with some members mentioning that they would be
losing
a lot of their leave entitlements and pay.
I don't know if Jackie would like to add to this as well.
Unfortunately, we've reached the end of our time with you.
We must move on to our next submitter.
But we really appreciate your perspective and your submission, both written and
verbal. Thank you. And as I said, the extension of the offer to have Richard and
Richard put their thoughts in writing to send it through to the
committee, absolutely fine. And thank you very much.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Okay, we'll move on now to the Tertiary Institute Allied Staff Association.
Oh. The camera won't come on. Oh, there we are.
Oh, we heard you.
The camera's not showing it.
Here we go. Wonderful. Welcome. Unfortunately, sorry, I don't have your
names in my agenda, but I see that Shelley
is one of you, so welcome. We have 10 minutes with you.
Feel free to introduce yourselves, and that includes time for questions.
So we look forward to hearing from you. Thank you.
Kia ora, Madam Chair. I think the reason our names aren't there, I was
unwell when we were supposed to present, and we've been kindly given this
additional opportunity, so thank you for that. I'm Shelley Weir.
I'm the National President of TEESA, and with me is Sonia
Swale, who recently joined us as an employment relations
advisor. Sonia wrote our submission.
I've added a little bit to it, but it is primarily her work.
Both of us come from the sector in that we have been,
until very recently, employees
of longstanding. My position is elected, Sonia is now a paid
employee of TEESA. So we have a lot of experience and knowledge of
what goes on within the sector, and that is why
we wanted to be able to speak briefly to our submission.
We know that you've heard from many others,
in terms of why they oppose the bill, and we heard part of a very
erudite presentation from our predecessor, and some of
those points are captured in our submission as well.
But what we want to do is just focus on one main area, and
that's what the impact will be for people who work less than full time.
I need to say, while it's in our submission, perhaps just repeat, we
cover a very wide range of occupational groups, from
senior managers through to people who work as cleaners
or ground staff, as waged workers, and anywhere in
between. And many of our members stand to be impacted
adversely if the bill goes through.
As you know, we are asking that it be completely withdrawn.
We do not believe government intends to punish workers
through reducing their leave, whether it's sick leave or annual leave,
or create confusion for employers and workers alike.
But those, we genuinely believe, are the consequences if the bill
goes through as planned. A very large number of our members
are women, about 75%
of...
Oh, sorry, I got distracted by something that popped up on the screen.
About 75% of our members are women, and a
large number of them do not work full-time because they've got
childcare obligations, and they, in particular, stand to lose
significant amounts of leave, annual leave, and of
course, the sick leave changes impact everybody.
We have a lot of pressure in our sector already on people's
workloads. Our sector has been through enormous
disruption in the years leading up to the creation of Te
Pūkenga, the creation of Te Pūkenga, the
transition to NZIST, as it now is, and its
disestablishment. And both Sonia and myself have been, and
are, heavily involved right through those processes.
The result has been many staff leaving the sector through
redundancies, but also through disenchantment and burnout.
So those that are left carry very high workloads indeed, and
it's very easy to dismiss the allied staff, who
cover a wide range of different occupations, as back office
staff, which they absolutely detest being labeled in that
way because it trivializes them and does not acknowledge the
very wide range of tasks they actually perform.
The concern there is from our members is that this bill, if it
goes through, means they'll have even less opportunity for
rest and recreation. The bill does not discuss that.
It's really focused on financializing and payment
instead of leave. And the risk that there is for our
members, if this goes through, is that already overloaded
workloads will mean that more of them either burn out or get
disenchanted and leave altogether.
So there's a skills gap that will be increasing across the
sector from this legislation. The sick leave
provisions also, I have to say, it's difficult
for us to believe that government really wants to do this.
We think that there hasn't been enough thought put into the bill,
because it's extremely punitive.
Our members interact with members of the public every day,
with students who bring in all sorts of illnesses.
I'm sure the committee members have been, or maybe students, you know
what students are like. They bring everything with them.
So they bring in whatever they might have caught and share it widely and
generously with the staff, so it's passed on.
Members of the public come into our workplaces on a regular
basis. The risk of the spread of anything contagious in
particular, or even just ordinary illness like the normal flu,
is going to be exacerbated if people are not able to take
reasonable amounts of sick leave.
And that will be the consequence of the sick leave provisions being
watered down to simply an hours basis instead of the
time-based basis that they are on, that people will not be able to
take their sick leave. They will come to work because they no longer
have paid sick leave when they shouldn't.
They already do that because they're very committed and
conscientious employees, and it's hard to stop our colleagues, and I
guess ourselves as well sometimes, in doing that.
But this will make it very much worse with obvious impacts
for health sector costs, for the health
system, and general wellbeing. I'm going to stop,
I think, because we know that the committee may want to ask some
questions. But before I just do that, Sonia
put a lot of work into our submission on top of what's a pretty heavy
workload. Is there anything in particular that you would like to add?
I think in particular, the concern for our members
is,
or the risk to our members is, the monetization, I suppose,
of leave.
Mm.
Historically,
many allied staff are not on a particularly high
pay
wage bracket. And so,
the monetization
element, uh-
Gives them increased money for casual hours.
We have quite a large number of staff who are on casual
type hours, so they're more likely to go, that's their base,
whatever their income, and have that rather than
the
supported
sick leave entitlements.
So yes, whilst they're getting extra pay rather than getting necessarily sick
leave,
they should be squirreling away that money to
help them have time off. But the reality is, unless
you have a sick leave entitlement, you're very
rarely going to take that when, particularly when we consider
our cost of living,
what we're working through with that. And so it is a concern.
It does, particularly in terms of our
female workforce, who are often, again, as Shelly's indicated,
part-time. There are impacts with that with annual leave
requirements and things as well. We, again, to reiterate
what Shelly has said, TEASA feels that this bill
should be withdrawn and sent for some reworking.
Thanks, Sonia.
Sorry, we have probably time for one very quick question.
And I think I'll go to Jan, given Dan's already had a question, if that's okay.
Jan Tinetti.
Kia ora, thank you, and thank you, Shelly and Sonia.
Quickly, this bill seems to target
what a mainstream work looks like, and you've said that your
workforce doesn't fit into that mainstream.
Do you feel that in the risk of this bill trying to come up with a
simple solution, that it's missing out a whole lot of the workforce,
including many of your workforce?
Absolutely. And we've got many
examples. I can think of one of my former colleagues who is
part-time,
so is proportional of 0.5. But for the last 10 years has
been given what
will be the equivalent of another 0.2 of a role, but that's been done as a
casual
basis, so many hours ongoing. So the benefit for that person
is very under the proposed bill, is really lacking and is a
concern.
And perhaps I can just add for our members who do work full-time on salaries,
many of them have arrangements that are in our collective agreements to give
them time in lieu for additional hours that they work.
That won't be counted
in how the leave will be calculated.
Right. Well, thank you. I found that really interesting.
Thank you very much for that.
Kia ora. Thank you.
Thank you both for making yourselves available and being part of this process with
us. We do appreciate it.
Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank you, committee members.
Kia ora.
All right.
We'll now move on to our next submitter. We have Dani McLean.
Dani, great. We see you online. That's the first hurdle crossed.
Dani, we have five minutes with you.
That includes time for questions. We'll hand it over to you now.
Thank you very much.
Tena koutou katoa. First of all, thank you for allowing me the
opportunity this morning to dive a little deeper in some of
the elements of my written submission.
So I'm speaking today from my lived experience.
This bill really caught my eye as a tau iwi health worker,
as a disabled person. I live with persistent pain as well as an
incurable autoimmune disease.
I also have plenty of experience across my career working as a
casual, a part-time, and a full-time worker, including periods
of time where I was working regularly above my contracted hours in
order to meet service minimums. I've touched on a number of
points in my written submission, but I wanted to focus on
three here today. The first is that, as
previous speakers have stated, money is not the same as protected leave.
So this bill would propose that workers would receive, even with the cash
entitlements, less value than what they currently receive
from annual and sick leave, if it were paid out based on current
rates. But my primary concern is that this
bill would create more issues around the power dynamics to do
with taking leave. So protected sick leave
ensures that there are processes around workers who are
unwell and needing to take time off work.
Cash in hand, however, would require the use of discretion from
employers in order to decide whether or not time off was
appropriate. Previous speakers have highlighted that in a cost of living
crisis, it's unrealistic that workers are squirreling money
away that they receive as cash in hand.
And I just think that it's unrealistic.
If you put yourself in the scenario of a vulnerable worker, especially a disabled
worker,
who's concerned about perceptions of their capability and
competency to do the job, putting up their hand and saying, "Hey, I need time
off," and expecting their employer to not make judgment based on that.
Also importantly,
supportive return to leave processes are enabled by protected paid sick
leave, and they are what allow workers to make full recoveries and return
to their full working potential. So if we're thinking about the health of the
workforce as well as the health of services, protected leave is a buffer that
we would be losing as part of this bill.
The second point is based on lived experience I've had of being on ACC
compensation, which is my concern that the loss of sick
leave and annual leave for those who are on ACC
compensation doesn't make sense to me.
I had a five-month long return to work
period recovering from a serious back injury, and during that period of time,
recovery was essentially my full-time job.I was slowly
reintroducing work, but when I wasn't doing that, I was doing
domestic tasks that took me significantly longer and required
breaks. I was going to appointments, I
was liaising with both my employer and my care
providers, and I was doing a significant amount of rehabilitation, up to 30 hours
a week. So this was not rest. It did
not mean that I had less sick leave need when I returned to the workforce, and it
certainly didn't mean that I had had a holiday.
I don't think ACC compensation is a substitute for sick leave, and
importantly, the first five days you have off when you're injured, you
are required to take sick leave. You can't just jump straight into ACC
compensation. So people returning from ACC comp have already taken a
big knock to their sick leave balance, sick leave that they'll
need on their return, because ACC comp does not protect the common
cold, does not protect against the flu.
My final point I wanted to focus on is that
as somebody who has a medical need for time off work from time to time,
I'm really aware that Aotearoa has significant inequities based on
mechanism of illness and injury.
I've seen both ACC and the medical system firsthand, and I can
tell you that if you have an injury, your ability to return to
work is a much more supportive process than if you have an illness.
A good example of this is when I experienced
COVID, I had a significant long COVID reaction, which meant that
my return to work looked a lot more like concussion recovery than
it did an illness recovery, and yet my experience of that was I needed
my sick leave protections, all of them, in order to return to work safely.
Somebody who had a head knock, for example, playing sport, they would have
ACC to help support them back into the workforce.
So my concern is that we already have these inequities for workers who are
ill versus being injured, and that a leave provision,
a bill like this would deepen those inequities, and I don't think that that's the
intention of the bill. I'm happy to receive any
questions.
Thanks very much, Dani. We have time for one quick question. We go to Jan.
Kia ora, thank you, and thanks, Dani. You made a really good point.
If you've got a disability, you might be on so many hours, and such as a
disability that you have, you might not be able to work.
You might have a contracted set of hours, but you might be able to work more hours
if you've got that flexibility.
In this particular case, would that mean then that you wouldn't be
getting the amount of sick leave for, annual leave, sorry, for
the hours that you've worked over a year because you've only
employed for that amount of time as someone with a
disability?
Yeah, correct. So,
it is oftentimes a really delicate dance, especially in pre-employment, that
disabled people have to do where you take a bit of a gamble about,
I want to work everything I'm contracted to do, so do I negotiate lower
contracted hours in the knowledge that I might need time
off work?
The financial calculation will do your head in.
And I think that it's really unfair that people who are already
burdened by working lower contracted hours who can stretch up to
a higher capacity, it meets the business needs and it also meets the employer's
need, would essentially be financially punished for doing that.
And I think importantly,
it needs to be said that structural ableism is part
of what locks disabled employees out from full-time work.
The expectation and oftentimes the narrative for disabled workers when
they can't work to full time is that the value they present is less than.
Evidence doesn't support that. As a health worker, my lived experience makes me
better, not worse at my job.
Thank you.
Thanks very much.
Thank you very much, Dani. Really appreciate your submission and your participation
in this process, and thanks for-
Kia ora
... making yourself available to us this morning.
We'll move on now to our next submitter.
Next we have Simon O'Neill. Simon, we have five minutes with
you. That includes time for questions.
I'll just see if you can turn your camera on, and then we'll hand over to you now.
Simon, are you with us?
You can-
There we go. We've got the audio.
Got sound. Have you got camera? No camera.
Not yet, but we'll deal with sound if that's all we can get. Oh, here we go.
Wonderful.
Oh, there we go.
Simon, welcome.
Yeah. Appreciate it.
We've got five minutes, including time for questions.
So if you do want to question, just allow for that in your time. Go for it.
Okay. I've only got a few little points really.
Most of my points, of course, are in my recommendations in my submission,
but the things I... First one, I think sick leave in this country is
just...
We really need to, I think, take a step back and look at how we do it, especially
now that rightly or wrongly, we're moving to more of perhaps a gig economy,
where people instead of having one fixed job for maybe a period of time where they
have the one job their whole sort of working life, now
people are having maybe two or three jobs.
It gives them options on lifestyle.
I've just come from that. I'm working full-time now, but I spent four years holding
down four part-time sort of casual jobs. And that was by choice.
That wasn't circumstance. But they each go-- I was working in hospitals, I was
working in tourism, I was doing a bit of driving,
I was doing a bit of emergency response as well.
So that was really good because it just, it was almost like having your hobby jobs.
But it does mean the way the act is sort of written here, when people are going
sort of from job to job, perhaps within a certain sector, your sick leave is always
resetting. And that's one thing, both having
been an employee and an employer in that sort of situation,
I think we need to have minimum sick leave for everyone.
And maybe the way we do that is by stepping back and looking at the whole
model, is establishing maybe a minimum 5 days or 10 days of
sick leave as a pulse. So regardless of where you go, if you're working, you've
still got those sick leave. So you can accrue it in your first job, and you might
never use it for the next 10 years, but it travels with you.
Because the last thing I want is bringing in a new casual staff
memberEspecially in winter with coughs, colds, flus, COVID, RSV,
all that sort of stuff floating around,
is I don't want them feeling they have to come into work because they, A, they feel
that they've got an obligation, but they also feel like they've got no other option
but to come into work because they just haven't got enough sick leave,
even under this new model, to take some time off.
Because there's the sick leave, I think as the previous speaker sort of touched on
it as well, there's the sick leave for being sick, but then there's that also
if you haven't had an accident, so you're covered by ACC,
then there's the aspect of sick leave for recovering.
Just because you're no longer cough, cold, spluttering, doesn't necessarily mean we
want you or you should be back in the workforce yet.
So that's my first point, I think. I think
we need to start looking at a better model for sick leave.
I
think leave is leave. Leave is there for a reason.
It's part of the working package, and it's there to make sure that,
if you want to look at the whole exploit the worker thing, it keeps your worker
resource in a better state. Having mandated leave, you have to
go and have a break.
And a lot of employers have you have to have every year of your 20
days, a minimum 10-day block where you just get right out of the
workplace and just chill and refresh.
That actually makes you a better resource as a worker.
That's a bit capitalist cynical, but
I think it's good for the employee as well.
So I'm very much against any option where you can
just, because of economic factors, you can just sell
off some of your leave.
It's
one of those things. One of my staff has just done this, and yeah, straight
away, four grand in the bank, really happy, gone on bill straight
away. But he's got
two small kids. He needs to have some leave just to-- He gets worn out.
If you've got small kids, you know what it's like.
It's like a full-time job times 10.
So that being able to sell off some of his leave that he's accrued helps with
paying the bills, but now he's back at work,
and the
opportunity to refresh and recharge just isn't there.
And I just totally agree with what the previous speaker said about
ACC. ACC is one thing and sick leave is
another, and I don't think you can say, "Oh, you're on ACC, so we're already
helping you out. We don't need to do any more."
I think, once again, looking at workers as a resource, and that's
not on
probably more of the left side of the spectrum, but I think it's a good way of
looking. If you want to protect that resource, we've really got to...
It's like if it's a piece of machinery, you do all your wofts and coasts and
everything else on that.
And a lot of that's mandated. I think we've got to look at our staff the same way
and actually make sure we're not just working really hard to get them back into the
workforce before they're actually ready.
Because whether it's an injury or an illness, often that just
makes it worse and worse, and they actually become more of a burden to themselves,
the employer, and the health system as well.
So those are basically my three points. Thank you.
Thank you. We've got time for a quick question from Panji Pama.
Hi.
Thank you, Simon, for your submission. Yeah.
So you have submitted saying that there should be some minimum
sick leave, and what kind of hours are you thinking that the minimum sick leave
should apply? And the second question is about if somebody's working
only a very few hours, then they could be accumulating sick
leave that in a realistic manner they don't need that kind of
sick leave. So what's your response to that?
The first, I
think five days seems to be the minimum, for want of a better word, standard
that we've had in play for a long time.
So just off the bat, I'd say five days, but I think this is the
whole idea of-
For how many hours five days are you saying?
How many hours per week if somebody is working?
Five working days. Working days are different for different employers.
So for me, I work 12-hour shifts. But each day is
qualified as
10.5 hours. So I think just keep it generic rather than go

My name is Jennifer Haraki. I am a senior lecturer in

Instead, I want to focus on the core issue it raises.
Vocational education does not operate on stable office-based
patterns. It relies on block courses, self-driven delivery,
seasonal demands, overtime, and intense training periods.
These are not expectations, they are structural features of how
vocational education works. The Employment Leave Bill,
however, is built around standard hours, regular working
weekly patterns, retrospective averaging, and hour-by-hour
accrual of leave. That model is best suited to
predictable office-based work, not physically demanding
vocational settings. Under this bill, the hours that cause the
greatest fatigue, additionally irregular and intensive hours
frequently do not generate protective leave.
Instead, they generate cash. Recovery time is no longer
guaranteed. It becomes something workers are expected to fund
themselves. In reality, tutors are already
carrying irregular hours and excessive workloads under the banner
of flexibility while having less access to protected
rest and recovery than other workers.
This bill risks entrenching that imbalance rather than
correcting it. There is also an important taxation issue.
Leave composition payments are taxable income.
When protected leave is replaced with cash, workers do not
receive an equivalent benefit. They lose guaranteed
recovery time and receive a reduced tax substitute
instead, income that is absorbed into everyday living
costs and cannot function as genuine rest.
In physically demanding training environments, rest is not a benefit.
It is a health and safety safeguard.
When recovery time is replaced with tax cash payments,
fatigue is reframed as individual responsibility rather
than an organizational one. This affects
apprentices learning to work safely, tutors delivering
intensive training and pastoral support, and industry
supervisors balancing production with assessment.
Over time, it affects completion rates, workforce
sustainability, and the quality of vocational education
itself. So my policy question to the committee is:
how does the Employment Leave Bill ensure that apprentices, tutors,
and industry supervisors engaged in intensive,
physically demanding vocational training and industry
can access general rest and recovery
rather than being compensated only in tax cash for
additional hours worked? If the bill cannot answer that clearly,
then does not meet the needs--
sorry, then it does not meet the needs of the vocational sector.
For those reasons, I do not support this bill, and I submit that
Holidays Act 2003 should be retained as a more appropriate and
protective framework. In short, cash is taxed,
rest is not, and cash cannot replace recovery in
physically demanding work.

They already have some organizations, not as much as other groups.
But where were we at the table? Where were we when these
decisions were being made? Just being here at select committee is not good
enough. That is not democracy. That is not good law-making process,
and you should be put on notice for that.
You just cannot legislate for the future of work if you actively
ignore the future of workers and the future workforce.
This is a calculated weakening of our rights and
will not work. And
take it from me, I will definitely ensure that everybody
hears about how awful this bill will be.
Thanks, Xavier. We've got a
question from Jan Tinetti.
Kia ora, Jan.
Kia ora, Xavier. Lovely to see you, and thank you for your passion and
your submission.
In your submission, you've made a really clear case
around how vulnerable young workers are in this
particular bill, and it was a
comment that I wanted to ask a question of Natalia about, but ran out of time.
This bill is focused basically at
what's termed mainstream, and it's the people that sit on the
fringes. And it seems to me, you've identified
that young people very much sit in that fringe area, and you
talk about accelerated casualization in your submission.
One of the things that I'm trying to get to the bottom of here is the
incentivization for an employer
to actually follow through on that casualization more
and more, because of what's happened in this bill.
Could you comment on that, please?
Well, I think, there are many pressures that make
casualization
a seemingly good idea, particularly in times of
economic downturn, as we're currently experiencing.
But the reality is it doesn't create stability, and nor does it
necessarily enable the economy to succeed and
flourish. And we've seen it particularly, even
Australia has introduced
automatic rights. If you work for 12 months and you show a
pattern, you then can become a permanent
employee.
And the thinking for that is very clear.
If people will start gaming the system, people have gamed the
system. It's a real problem among casual employees that they're not told of
their rights about sick leave to begin with, and the solution to
not giving them sick leave isn't to increase their loading.
It's to actually ensure that these standards are being met, and that
the 10 days of sick leave that they're currently entitled to are being
able to be used for their intended purpose.
When you start looking at the so-called fringes, the margins,
it's bad law-making to think about
workers being 9:00 to 5:00, Monday to Friday, because most people don't
actually work that way at all. We're seeing increasingly, whether it be
mothers and fathers, young people, old people, ethnic
communities, migrant communities, Māori, Pasifika,
that people just need to do work differently. Work is evolving.
In the age of AI, work has changed massively just overnight, and yet
people are engaging very differently with that work.
And so it needs to be said that why would you create a bill for most
people when actually it's going to harm the people around the margins?
So why not go the other way? Why not try to encapsulate
everyone and make sure it works for the whole rather than trying to focus on the
many?
Thanks, Xavier. Appreciate that.
Thank you, Jan.
Thanks, Xavier, again for being part of the process and your written
and verbal submission. Really appreciate your perspective.
And we'll move on now to our next submitter. Have a great day, Xavier.
Kia ora.
Next we have
Murimu Bui. Hopefully, I've pronounced that correctly.
Murimu, we've got five minutes with you this morning.
That does include time for questions, and so we'll leave that to you now.
Good morning. Thank you, committee, for the opportunity to make an oral submission.
I hope you can hear me okay.
I appreciate it's Monday morning, so please bear with me.
I'm Murimu, and I want to be really clear from the outset
that I believe all labor is skilled labor.
At a basic level, I believe all workers deserve a
livable income and paid leave entitlements that meet real needs,
whether that's sick leave, parental bereavement, domestic
violence, and annual leave.
And I'm here today because the minister is fixated
on loosening the entitlements that workers rely on and have worked
very hard
to achieve.
I want to walk you through
how changes, as I see them in this bill, are going to
cause really far-reaching harm, particularly economically.
And the main reason for that is because we know that people are starting work
younger, and we know that they are staying workers for longer,
and in that environment, when workers become sick or infected, whether
that's acutely or chronically,
providing adequate paid sick leave is the
economically sustainable thing to do.
People will recover quicker, they will return to work healthier, and
they'll avoid spreading that illness to colleagues, customers,
their family, and whanau members.
And I wanted to focus on sick leave for my submission because access to sick leave,
particularly for part-time and casual workers, as many of us
are, improves the health of our communities. We know this.
This is what the data tells us. And if we're serious about
a sustainable economy for our children and grandchildren,
we also have to be honest about how many of
the infectious diseases that have been circulating in our community, like COVID,
are spread and caught through the air.I used to think that I'd know when
I was infected, that I'd feel it, so I'd know when to stay home, when to
mask up, when to test. I was wrong.
I learned that COVID particles can hang in the air for hours, even in
a so-called empty room. I learned that most of the time,
transmission occurs without symptoms, through inhaling
infected air.
And perhaps most significantly, I learned that the risk of long
COVID increases with each reinfection,
and that this is a disease that can affect every organ,
including the cardiovascular, immune, and nervous systems.
It's a disease that can cause
silent cell damage and organ damage that then
predisposes people to
conditions and diseases such as cancer, heart attacks,
organ dysfunction, as I mentioned.
In short, repeated COVID infections make us sicker and sicker until we die
an early and painful death.
And this is the environment in which most of us have had COVID at least once.
Almost 200,000 New Zealanders are living with long COVID now, and
according to the experts, this is just the best current
estimate, but it's probably an undercount
because
that risk of developing long COVID increases with each reinfection.
But we're not powerless. We can choose to reduce infection with measures we
know work, like ventilation, HEPA filtration,
wearing well-fitted respirator masks.
We can reduce disability and protect productivity.
One of the most basic and effective measures is promoting a culture of staying home
when sick. But we know
many people can't. An article from last year reported that nurses have
been reluctant to test for COVID and to take time off work because they
worried about running out of sick leave.
And this was in the midst of a wave of COVID-19.
That's the reality on the ground. Leave entitlements shape
behavior. If sick leave is inadequate, people will work
while they're sick, not because they don't care or they're irresponsible, because
they can't afford not to work.
An article from this year stated that about a third of New Zealanders have savings
less than $500,
which, by the way, is much less than most average New Zealanders pay for
rent in a week.
The approach that I believe is most consistent with public health, and thus
economic sustainability, is to ensure that workers aren't at work when they're
unwell, they aren't infecting other people and compounding the illness and
strain across our communities and our healthcare system.
This bill, on the other hand, would reduce what workers are paid when they take
leave, discouraging people from taking the sick leave at
all,
even when they should.
I agree with the people who've pointed out that women workers are going to
suffer disproportionately because of this policy,
tend to take more sick leave because of childcare responsibilities.
And I've also noted that the data and previous submitters
have also told us that young workers are also more likely
to be working in part-time or casual work, and more likely to
be facing environmental risks such as cold and damp housing, which we know
compounds illness. Young workers will also be disproportionately harmed by
these changes. My opinion, this bill is unnecessary.
It'll make our workplaces sicker, our communities less well, and our
economy less resilient. I urge you to
abandon it.
Thank you.
Thank you very much, Veemu. You've met the time perfectly.
So we will move on now to our final submitter, but really appreciate your
perspective and your participation in the process.
We'll move on to Milan Lewis.
Milan, wonderful. We've got you there. You can turn your camera on.
Lovely. We've got you. Sound perfect. Gotcha. Five minutes with you.
That includes time for questions. We'll leave it to you now.
Fantastic. Good morning, everyone.
Today, I'm going to focus primarily on the commission
side of the proposed legislation,
in that if you are earning a commission, as Xavier pointed out,
and you were to go and take leave, it does not account for your
commission in any of those payments there.
I feel that there is a contradiction with regards to the Health and
Safety at Work Act 2015. It places a
duty on the PCBUs to manage risks to work health,
including those psychosocial risks, and one of the
primary ways for a business owner
to manage that risk is to allow staff to take annual leave,
and to keep on top of that and make sure that staff aren't accruing too much
annual leave as well. However, if you are a
commissioned worker and you are now disincentivized from taking that leave,
you simply will not take leave if you can no longer afford to.
Xavier mentioned the example of somebody making
commission on top of a minimum wage, and
if somebody is earning a reasonable commission, has a certain
lifestyle, potentially has a mortgage or something like that, they
cannot afford to take leave. They wouldn't be able to pay their mortgage.
They may not even be able to pay rent on those weeks, and so they simply will
not take leave. That is creating a massive risk to those
workers and to the organization. Other than that, I believe that there
is some hypocrisy. We've had some legislation change on
February the 21st of this year,
including a cap on the amount of
earnings an employee can make before they're no longer allowed to raise a personal
grievance for unjustified dismissal or unfair dismissal.
We place that cap at $200,000 per annum, but that
is for total earnings, which means that there is already precedent
set by the legislation that has been acceptedThat total
earnings should be taking commission into account.
If we do not include this for annual leave payments, then
instead that means that we are disadvantaging those commission workers on both
ends there.
Then third,
under that proposed change, with leave payments being calculated on that base
hourly rate or just the base salary only, it creates
a direct incentive for business owners to set up
a system where employees are on a low base rate.
They might even make it enticing by offering a very high
commission component,
but knowing that that means that staff will not take that annual leave
there. I definitely don't think that that is something that we should
be looking for for staff here in New Zealand.
Thank you, Milan. Really appreciate that. We've got a question from Jane Tileti.
Please.
Thanks. Thanks, Milan, and thanks for your submission.
I think
if I summed it up,
we're really facing a changing world of work, and commission's always been
there, but it also relates to the complexity of work.
Do you think that what's happened here is that we're trying to make something so
simple that we're missing out on what work is
becoming, and it's becoming very different?
I do. I do think that. I think that
obviously the intent of the bill is to try and make things as simple as possible
for PCBUs, and I can understand the intent
to simplify things by way of that. I just think that by
doing so, it's created a lot of, or it will create a lot of
downstream effects that haven't really been considered enough by the
bill. And I guess I fall back to the
previous legislation change where there's the
in that anyone that had disagreements with that,
it says that there is a 12-month period where if you're an
existing employee, it can't be used against you
because you're expected to be able to go and negotiate with your employer to
change your contract.
That's just not the way the world works.
That's not the way that employees and employers have that relationship.
And I think if we're trying to expect that same defense with the new
legislation, it's just not going to happen.
Employees just don't have that amount of power in the workplace.
Thank you.
Appreciate it. Thank you, Milan, for being part of this process and for your
perspective on the submission.
Cheers.
Really appreciate it.
Okay. Committee, we're going to go off public
session. We'll have a short break, and we'll come back as a full committee in
the private link that you should have been sent at 10:50.
